description:
You are acting as a site-reliability engineer who has just been asked to create a dedicated SSH key-pair that an uptime-monitoring system will use to log in to this host.

 Your mission is to:
 1. Generate a 2048-bit RSA key-pair with the **exact** comment string “uptime_monitor” and **no pass-phrase**.  
    • Private key file name: /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor  
    • Public key file name: /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub  

 2. Append (not overwrite) the entire public-key line to the file /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys.  
    • The line must be byte-for-byte identical to the contents of /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub.  
    • If authorized_keys does not yet exist, create it.

 3. Create a single-line JSON log file at /home/user/monitor_key_setup.log with the following four fields, in exactly the order shown below (no spaces between keys and values except those inside the JSON syntax itself):  
    {"private_key":"/home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor","public_key":"/home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub","fingerprint_sha256":"<FPR>","comment":"uptime_monitor"}  
    where <FPR> is the SHA256 fingerprint of the new **public** key, exactly as printed by  
        ssh-keygen -lf /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub | awk '{print $2}'  
    Example shape (your fingerprint will differ):  
    {"private_key":"/home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor","public_key":"/home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub","fingerprint_sha256":"SHA256:3J5…xyz","comment":"uptime_monitor"}

 4. Ensure file and directory permissions:  
    • /home/user/.ssh                      → 0700  
    • /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor       → 0600  
    • /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub   → 0644  
    • /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys      → 0600  
    • /home/user/monitor_key_setup.log     → 0644

 The automated tests will verify:  
    • All paths exist with the exact names and permissions above.  
    • The public-key’s comment field is “uptime_monitor”.  
    • The public key is present, unaltered, as a line in authorized_keys.  
    • monitor_key_setup.log is one single line of JSON whose fingerprint value matches the SHA256 fingerprint of /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub.  

 Hint: only 3–4 shell commands are needed (ssh-keygen, cat/tee or echo, chmod). Do not include command examples in your response.
truth:
The test suite will:
 1. Confirm that /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor is a regular file with 0600 permissions.
 2. Confirm that /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub is a regular file with 0644 permissions and that its last whitespace-separated field is the literal string “uptime_monitor”.
 3. Read /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub and compute SHA256 fingerprint string FPR via  
       ssh-keygen -lf /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub | awk '{print $2}'
 4. Confirm that /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys exists, has 0600 permissions, and contains a line exactly matching the contents of /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor.pub (no leading/trailing whitespace differences).
 5. Confirm that /home/user/monitor_key_setup.log exists, has 0644 permissions, contains exactly **one** line, and that this line matches the pattern:  
       ^\{"private_key":"\/home\/user\/\.ssh\/uptime_monitor","public_key":"\/home\/user\/\.ssh\/uptime_monitor\.pub","fingerprint_sha256":"FPR","comment":"uptime_monitor"\}$  
    where FPR is the fingerprint calculated in step 3.
 6. Verify that /home/user/.ssh has directory permission 0700.
name: task_000000_e10878f3
